title: ReLU
description: A pointwise activation that keeps positive FFN values and clamps negative ones to zero.
openingSummary: ReLU is a simple FFN activation that keeps positive hidden values and snaps negative ones to zero, giving transformer MLPs a cheap way to decide which features stay active.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: ReLU stands for rectified linear unit. Inside a standard FFN, it looks at each hidden value after the expand layer and applies one rule: keep positive numbers, replace negative numbers with zero. The FFN stays in the same slot after attention, but this activation changes which hidden features survive before the block projects back to model width.

whatItOptimizes.title: What It Optimizes
whatItOptimizes.body: ReLU optimizes for a very cheap, very sharp nonlinearity. It creates sparse hidden activity because only positive features pass through fully, which made it a strong early baseline for dense FFNs.

practicalBenefit.title: Practical Benefit
practicalBenefit.body: Once you recognize ReLU, it becomes easier to spot what later activation variants are changing. Many newer activations keep the same FFN slot and the same dense path, then only soften or gate the activation step.

howItWorks.title: How It Works
howItWorks.body: The token state enters the wide hidden layer, then ReLU acts on each hidden coordinate separately. Positive values keep their size. Negative values become zero. After that, the FFN projects the filtered hidden state back to model width.

mathOrComputeSchema.title: Math Or Compute Schema
mathOrComputeSchema.body: The formula below shows ReLU as the one-coordinate rule inside the wider FFN. The key detail is the hard cutoff at zero.

comparedToNearbyModules.title: Compared To Nearby Modules
comparedToNearbyModules.body: ReLU is the sharpest cutoff in this small activation family. LeakyReLU preserves a faint negative path, while SiLU smooths the whole transition instead of using a hard corner.

exampleArchitectures.title: Example Architectures
exampleArchitectures.body: ReLU mattered most in earlier dense networks and remains a common baseline in papers, even when the largest language models now favor smoother or gated FFN activations.

limitationsAndTradeoffs.title: Limitations And Tradeoffs
limitationsAndTradeoffs.body: Because every negative value becomes zero, ReLU can discard weak negative evidence completely. That simplicity helps efficiency, but it also makes the activation less smooth than newer alternatives.

whyItStillMatters.title: Why It Still Matters
whyItStillMatters.body: ReLU remains the cleanest reference point for activation discussions. When a paper claims an FFN activation is smoother, leakier, or more selective, ReLU is usually the baseline hiding underneath that comparison.

computeFlow.alt: Activation comparison graph showing how ReLU, LeakyReLU, and SiLU change the hidden activation step inside the same FFN slot
computeFlow.caption: Switch between nearby activation rules to see how the same FFN path changes once hidden values cross below zero

comparisonTable.caption: How ReLU compares with nearby activation variants

relu.negativeBranch: Clamp every negative hidden value to 0
relu.mainTradeoff: Very simple and sparse, but it throws away all negative signal

leakyRelu.negativeBranch: Keep a small slope alpha x for x < 0
leakyRelu.transitionShape: Hard corner with a weak negative path
leakyRelu.mainTradeoff: Preserves gradient on the negative side, but stays less smooth than SiLU

silu.negativeBranch: Shrink negative values smoothly with x sigma(x)
silu.mainTradeoff: Smoother hidden filtering, but less minimal than ReLU

x.definition: One hidden value inside the expanded FFN state.

negativeBehavior.label: Negative hidden values stop here: x < 0 becomes 0 before projection back
